---
pagetitle: "fmrimod"
toc: false
sidebar: false
page-layout: full
jupyter: python3
---

::: {.hero}
# fmrimod

[Typed, composable fMRI design and statistical modeling in Python.]{.lead}

[Get started](get-started.qmd){.btn .btn-primary}
[Browse the API](reference/index.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-secondary}
:::

## What fmrimod Is

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
::: {.card .h-100 .p-3}
### Typed
Express HRFs, baselines, contrasts, AR options, single-trial estimators, and group reductions as inspectable Python values.
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
::: {.card .h-100 .p-3}
### Composable
Use the load-bearing workflow `fmri_dataset -> fmri_lm -> contrast -> group_fit`; low-level matrix helpers stay secondary.
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
::: {.card .h-100 .p-3}
### Parity-Validated
Treat R behavior, Nilearn, and FitLins as references, then prove agreement through benchmark and cross-testing artifacts.
:::
:::

:::

`fmrimod` is the unified Python home for the `fmri*` R modeling ecosystem, but
it is not a mechanical translation. The public API is redesigned around typed
composition, reproducible analysis objects, and workflow parity that can be
checked against concrete artifacts.

## In 30 Seconds

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import fmrimod as fm

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
events = pd.DataFrame({
    "onset": [0, 12, 24, 36],
    "condition": ["face", "scene", "face", "scene"],
    "duration": [2, 2, 2, 2],
})

data = rng.normal(size=(40, 8))
dataset = fm.fmri_dataset(data, tr=1.0, events=events)
fit = fm.fmri_lm("hrf(condition)", dataset)

fit.n_coefficients, fit.n_voxels
```

[Read the full tutorial](get-started.qmd)
